title: The AI Economist: Optimal Economic Policy Design via Two-level Deep Reinforcement Learning
study_design_description: The study validates an AI-driven economic policy design approach in simplified one-step economies and a more complex Gather-Trade-Build economy, a two-dimensional, spatiotemporal economy with agents who move, gather resources, trade, and build houses. Each simulation runs for 10 tax years, with each tax year lasting 100 timesteps (H = 1000 total). Experiments use two spatial layouts (Open-Quadrant and Split-World) and are repeated with either 5 or 10 random seeds depending on the scenario.
primary_task_description: Agents choose labor amounts to optimize post-tax utility based on their skill level and tax rates. Agents can move spatially to collect resources (stone and wood), trade with one another through a continuous double auction, and build houses by spending resources to earn coins. The planner sets and changes tax rates at the start of each tax year to maximize social welfare.
population_description: The one-step economy includes N = 100 agents, each with a private skill level (hourly wage). The Gather-Trade-Build scenarios include four Split-World scenarios with 10 agents each (differing in resource-rich half assignment) and two Open-Quadrant scenarios with 4 and 10 agents respectively. All agents have fixed build-skills calibrated so that agents must be strategic in earning income and the resulting income distribution roughly matches the 2018 US economy.

arms.description: A fixed-rate planner where all tax rates are 0%
A fixed-rate planner where bracketed marginal tax rates follow a progressive scheme adapted from the 2018 US federal single-filer income tax schedule
An adaptive planner that computes theoretically optimal marginal rates using the empirical income distribution and elasticity of income with respect to taxation
A deep neural network, adaptive planner that maps a set of planner observations to bracketed marginal tax rates, which is trained via reinforcement learning (RL) to maximize social welfare

outcomes.outcome_description: A utilitarian objective defined as a family of linear-weighted sums of agent utilities with inverse-income weights: swft = ∑ᵢ ωᵢ·uᵢ,ₜ, where ωᵢ ∝ 1/Cᵢ, normalized to sum to 1
Equality defined as eq(Cₜ) = 1 − N/(N−1) gini(Cₜ), where the Gini index is gini(Cₜ) = ∑ᵢ ∑ⱼ |Cᵢ,ₜ−Cⱼ,ₜ| / (2N∑ᵢ Cᵢ,ₜ), with 0 ≤ eq(Cₜ) ≤ 1 and 0 ≤ gini(Cₜ) ≤ N−1/N
Productivity defined as the sum of all incomes: prod(Cₜ) = ∑ᵢ Cᵢ,ₜ
An objective that optimizes a trade-off between equality and productivity, defined as the product of equality and productivity: swfₜ = eq(Cₜ)·prod(Cₜ)
Income earned by low build-skill agents from trading
Wealth transfers from the highest build-skill agent to others
Number of incomes in the second-to-highest bracket (204 to 510 coins)
Number of incomes in the top bracket

notes: This is a computational study using two-level deep reinforcement learning to design economic policies in simulated multi-agent economies. The paper compares four policy approaches: Free Market (no taxes), US Federal (fixed progressive schedule), Saez tax (theoretically optimal adaptive), and AI Economist (learned adaptive policy). Open-Quadrant experiments used 10 random seeds; Split-World and One-Step Economy experiments used 5 random seeds. Statistical significance was computed using two-sample t-tests at α = 0.05, 0.001, and 0.00001 levels. The paper includes extensive caveats about the limitations of the simulations for real-world policy application, noting that the environments do not capture the full range of economic opportunities and decisions faced by real-world individuals, and that results are not meant to be applied to specific real-world economies. The authors acknowledge challenges in measuring local elasticities and potential risks of bias in AI-driven policy systems.
